query: hypertensive hemorrhage common site in basal ganglia

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Basal Ganglia -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The putamen is considered to be the most common site of intracerebral hemorrhage secondary to systemic hypertension. Surgical evacuation of such bleeds is still a topic of debate. Open craniotomy and endoscopic methods have been used to evacuate such bleeds in indicated cases. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracerebral Hemorrhage -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary or spontaneous ICH accounts for over 85% of hemorrhagic strokes. [3] A primary ICH diagnosis is often one of exclusion where no other pathological or structural cause is found and is supported by a history of chronic hypertension, increased age, and location of the clot. In patients with chronic arterial hypertension, it is thought that lipohyalinosis and degenerative changes of penetrating arterioles result in Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms in the small arterial vessels supplying deep cerebral structures. [1] Over 60% of primary bleeds are related to hypertension, and these hematomas are most commonly seen in the posterior fossa, pons, basal ganglia, and thalamus. [1] Lobar hemorrhages in older patients are often the distinguishing feature of amyloid angiopathy. This is a degenerative disease, thought to be related to alleles of the apolipoprotein E gene, allowing for increased amyloid deposition within vessel walls. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Nucleus Caudate -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Lesions of the caudate nucleus, such as hemorrhages or strokes, can have widespread effects depending upon their extent and the potential involvement of other nearby tissues. The caudate nucleus is not a common site of hemorrhage, but patients with caudate nucleus hemorrhage present with symptoms suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhages such as headache, emesis, and nuchal rigidity. [28] Most hemorrhagic strokes of the caudate nucleus occur due to hypertension, though rarely rupture of internal carotid artery aneurysms or arteriovenous malformations can precipitate them. [4] Other strong risk factors for caudate nucleus vascular lesions include hypercholesterolemia and diabetes. [28] Following hemorrhage, patients commonly experience symptoms such as ocular motor deficits, dysarthria, and abulia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Basal Ganglia -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Blood supply to the basal ganglia is primarily supplied by the lenticulostriate arteries, which are branches of the middle cerebral artery. Note that the lenticulostriate arteries are prone to hemorrhage in patients with uncontrolled hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Putamen -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The putamen is a common site for hypertensive bleed as well as infarction. The corkscrew pattern of lenticulostriate vessels (increases intraluminal pressure), as well as the formation of Charcot-Bouchard aneurysm secondary to fibrinoid necrosis, predisposes to its rupture. [24] [30] On the contrary, lipohyalinosis and micro-atheroma formation account for resulting in infarction. [24] Bilateral putaminal hemorrhages, though rare, can occur in cases of bleeding disorders, methanol intoxication, metastatic lesions, and amyloid angiopathies. There can be a multispectral presentation in cases with small putaminal strokes, and these can categorize as follows [24] : Mixed motor and sensory Pure motor Pure sensory Ataxic hemiparesis Dysarthria with clumsy hands Hemiballism and hemichorea

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Putamen -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The putamen, as a common structure affected by a hypertensive cerebral hemorrhage, elicits a large range of presentations based on the magnitude of the initial blood extravasation. [24] Despite the controversy, surgical evacuation of the intracerebral hemorrhage is a mainstay therapeutic approach to decompress the mass effect and also eliminate the cytotoxic edema resulting from the ischemia and the degraded blood products. [25] Current recommendations to remove putaminal bleed include large size hematoma with life-threatening herniation, especially in young aged patients. [26] The other indication of removal of hematoma includes the presence of hemiparesis owing to the compression of the internal capsule from the hematoma that can be confirmed by the application of the MR tractography study (see Image. Coronal Section of Brain Through Anterior Commissure). [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Pathophysiology -- Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: Non-traumatic intraparenchymal hemorrhage often occurs secondary to hypertensive damage to cerebral blood vessels, which eventually burst and bleed into the brain. Other causes include rupture of an AVM, rupture of an aneurysm, arteriopathy, tumor, infection, or venous outflow obstruction. Penetrating and non-penetrating trauma may also cause intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The most common location of a spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is the putamen, which is usually associated with uncontrolled hypertension. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebellar Hematoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common non-traumatic etiology for cerebellar hemorrhage as for all intracranial hemorrhages is hypertensive vasculopathy causing microaneurysms and resultant rupture. Cerebellar hemorrhage from hypertension most commonly occurs in the middle-aged or older population, often in the dentate nucleus with extension to other areas from the rupture of microaneurysms along the penetrating small vessels. Other causes of cerebellar hemorrhage include trauma, coagulopathy (cirrhosis, disseminated intravascular coagulation or thrombocytopenia) or bleeding disorders (primary and drug-induced (anticoagulation, antiplatelet or thrombolytic medications)), ischemic stroke with hemorrhagic transformation, cerebral amyloid angiopathy, [2] vascular malformations such as arteriovenous malformation (AVM), dural arteriovenous fistula in the tentorium, [3] brain tumors (primary or metastatic), [4] [5] septic embolism, central nervous system infection (encephalitis, moyamoya disease, vasculitis (lupus)), sympathomimetic drugs such as cocaine and amphetamines, [6] aneurysm (posterior circulation) or remote hemorrhage following supratentorial neurosurgical procedure. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Stroke -- Etiology -- Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH)
retrieved_docs.content: ICH stands as the second most prevalent form of stroke. ICH is typically caused by the rupture of small arteries, often due to hypertensive vasculopathy, cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA), coagulopathies, and other vasculopathies. Hypertensive vasculopathy is predominantly associated with non-lobar ICH (in regions such as the basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, and brainstem), whereas CAA is more commonly linked to lobar ICH. [15] Several risk factors contribute to ICH, including advancing age, hypertension, CAA, smoking, excessive alcohol intake, sympathomimetic drugs, anticoagulants, and antiplatelet drugs. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Duret Hemorrhages -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The most common differential diagnosis includes primary brainstem hemorrhages, which are petechial in appearance and represent hypertensive bleeds. Radiologically, Duret hemorrhages have a linear configuration that extends in the brainstem from a ventral and dorsal location, but may be of any shape in the clinical setting of supratentorial abnormalities that cause transtentorial herniation. Primary hypertensive hemorrhages are usually larger and occur spontaneously in patients with an antecedent history of uncontrolled hypertension without supratentorial abnormalities. Petechial hemorrhages are multifocal and small in size and are seen around the dorsal midbrain in periaqueductal and tectal locations in cases with diffuse axonal injuries following TBI. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Crisis -- Etiology -- Central nervous system disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Head injury Cerebral infarction Intracerebral hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: Hemiballismus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Excessive dopaminergic activity and damage to the pathways of the basal ganglia (hyperkinetic theory) are the basic predominant etiologies of hyperkinetic syndromes. Following are some of the common etiologies of hemiballismus: Intracranial hemorrhage [4] Stroke (ischemic): the most common cause in patients more than 65-year-old from the small perforating branches of the basilar artery [1] Neoplasm Head trauma Metabolic (e.g., nonketotic hyperglycemic hyperosmolar syndrome in uncontrolled type 2 diabetics) [5] Neuroinfectious: although rare, are more common in younger patients Tuberculomas [6] Toxoplasmosis: from complications of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) given its predilection to cause lesions in the basal ganglia Neuroinflammatory diseases: such as demyelinating disorders (e.g., multiple sclerosis) [7] Paraneoplastic syndromes [8] Vasculitis (e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus) [9] Toxic: alcohol, heavy metals exposure

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebellar Hematoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cerebellar location is not unique as far as the causes of intracranial hemorrhage. The prevailing belief is that patients with long-standing hypertension have degenerative changes in the penetrating small blood vessel walls leading to the subsequent formation of microaneurysms that rupture and hemorrhage. [13] Tumors, blood disorders, amyloid, arteriovenous malformation, trauma, and stimulant drug abuse may also lead to cerebellar hemorrhage. Remote cerebellar hemorrhage after craniotomies is thought to be a result of a loss of cerebral spinal fluid (CSF), causing a "sag" in the cerebellum with resultant venous bleeding often tracking along the superior cerebellum with most cases having a benign course. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Evaluation -- Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: Other imaging to consider include CTA, MRA, or diagnostic cerebral angiogram to look for cerebrovascular causes of the intraparenchymal hemorrhage. Diagnostic cerebral angiography is indicated for all patients with ICH except those aged more than 45 with preexisting hypertension in thalamic, putaminal, or posterior fossa hemorrhage. [14] Cavernomas are angiographically occult and can be picked up incidentally or present with small parenchymal hemorrhage resulting in headaches, neurological deficits, or seizures. They have a characteristic "popcorn" or "berry" appearance on MRI scans with a rim of signal loss due to haemosiderin (see Image. Popcorn-Like Appearance of a Cavernoma). Different intensities of blood products in different sequences of MRI scans [15] :

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Cavernous Angiomas(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy: older adults, usually numerous small foci located in brain periphery Chronic hypertensive encephalopathy: hemorrhagic foci  more common in the basal ganglia Diffuse axonal injury (DAI): a history of trauma, unconsciousness Cerebral vasculitis Radiation-induced vasculopathy Hemorrhagic metastases: metastatic melanoma, thyroid, renal cell, choriocarcinoma Multiple hemorrhages associated with blood dyscrasia (disseminated intravascular coagulopathy, leukemia) Occult vascular malformation (thrombosed AVM/aneurysm, capillary telangiectasia) Hemorrhagic primary neoplasm

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Internal Capsule -- Clinical Significance -- Hemorrhagic Strokes Involving the Internal Capsule
retrieved_docs.content: Severe hemorrhage involving the internal capsule, subcortical connections between the cerebral cortex, and other subcortical nuclei may be damaged. Moreover, some of the nuclei themselves may be destroyed. The interruption of other descending tracts from these subcortical centers would produce the following clinical signs as mentioned above: (a) severe paralysis on the opposite side of the body; (b) spasticity of the paralyzed muscles; (c) exaggerated deep muscle reflexes on the opposite side of the body to the lesion (clonus may be demonstrated); and (d) clasp knife reaction, which may be felt In the paralyzed muscles. [24]

Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: The vascular system is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease, indicating that these cerebrovascular conditions involve abnormalities or pathology of the blood vessels that supply the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is the anatomical region implicated in various cerebrovascular and arterial disorders. It is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease, reflecting a range of poorly characterized cerebrovascular pathologies that involve the brain’s blood vessels. Additionally, the Vascular System is the site of تسلخ الشرايين, an arterial ulceration condition that denotes damage to the arterial wall. Recognizing the Vascular System as the common anatomical substrate for these conditions underscores its central role in the pathophysiology of cerebrovascular disease and arterial injury, informing diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1181
retrieved_docs.content: excess blood pressure), highlighting hypertensive encephalopathy as another related condition. “Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten” itself is positioned under the parent term “other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease,” reinforcing its status as a collection of poorly defined or miscellaneous cerebrovascular pathologies. Collectively, these relationships depict a structured network in which stroke serves as a central clinical event linked to various vascular abnormalities—such as cerebral artery sclerosis, arterial dissection, and hypertensive brain injury—each situated within broader taxonomic groups that span circulatory, psychosomatic, and ill‑defined cerebrovascular domains. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in categorizing cerebrovascular disorders, recognizing overlapping risk factors, and guiding comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 0 -- Community 14
retrieved_docs.content: (e.g., “bloeding; intracerebraal, foetus of pasgeborene”) and directly to “نزيف مخي وليدي”, underscoring that traumatic birth can precipitate serious intracranial hemorrhage. Consequently, extreme prematurity, birth‑related trauma leading to intracranial hemorrhage, and perinatal infections constitute interrelated components of neonatal morbidity, informing clinicians about the spectrum of conditions to consider when evaluating and managing newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a spectrum of cerebrovascular disorders classified under “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System.” Cerebral infarction (الاحتشاء) results from vascular occlusion (انسداد الأوعية الدموية) of precerebral arteries, whether caused by embolism, thrombosis, or basilar‑artery occlusion/stenosis. Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) is a form of arterial narrowing (تضيق الشرايين) that may be asymptomatic, producing only transient systemic signs such as syncope (غشي) and affecting general wellbeing. These occlusive processes are linked to chronic arterial changes such as cerebral arterial sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) and intracranial arterial sclerosis, which in turn predispose to acute cerebrovascular accidents (حادثة وعائية دماغية), a child of transient cerebral ischemia (نوبة إقفارية عابرة) and a member of the SMQ “أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي.” Hypertension‑related brain damage (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم) is included among other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten). Anatomically, the structure of the intracranial artery serves as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان) of head‑and‑neck vessels, a surgical intervention that addresses cases of arterial thrombosis and embolism (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية). Specific entities such as basilar‑artery narrowing (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي), basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), and basilar‑artery embolism (Embolie; arteria basilaris) are mapped to the same occlusion/stenosis category and are inverse‑isa of cerebral artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي). The vascular system is also the anatomic site for renal vessel disorders (اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية) and arterial ulceration (تسلخ الشرايين). Cerebral arterial sclerosis is associated with multiple sclerotic atrophic changes (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 541
retrieved_docs.content: for both other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease and arterial ulceration. The group “Other cerebrovascular diseases” (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten) includes dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture and hypertensive‑related brain pathology, serving as a child of the English‑language classification “other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease.” Routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) often incorporates blood‑pressure control to mitigate these interrelated cardiovascular and neurological complications. Partial thyroidectomy is noted to co‑occur with دراق, suggesting a possible clinical association.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 691
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific type of birth trauma. In the context of neonatal and fetal health, birth trauma is mapped to intracerebral bleeding that can occur in the fetus or newborn. Consequently, when a birth trauma event leads to intracerebral hemorrhage, it may result in a cerebral injury that is directly attributable to the traumatic birth process. Understanding this link highlights the importance of recognizing and managing intracerebral bleeding in newborns as a potential cause of cerebral injury stemming from birth trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2445
retrieved_docs.content: Other cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten) comprise a range of distinct conditions, including dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture (Dissectie van cerebrale arteriën zonder ruptuur) and brain injury caused by excessive blood pressure (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم). Both of these entities are classified as child conditions of the broader category of other cerebrovascular diseases, illustrating the diversity of non‑hemorrhagic vascular pathologies that can affect the brain. Recognizing these specific subtypes within Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted therapeutic strategies in cerebrovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: System” is identified as the common substrate for other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten), renal vascular disorder, and arterial dissection, and serves as the parent for the specific brain injury caused by excess blood pressure (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم) within the cerebrovascular disease hierarchy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how essential hypertension integrates with renal, cerebral, and systemic vascular pathologies, informing comprehensive clinical assessment and integrated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 535
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم) is presented as a primary disease that directly causes chronic kidney disease due to hypertension and exerts systemic effects on the autonomic nervous system (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل). Elevated blood pressure is linked to a spectrum of central nervous system vascular diseases (أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي (SMQ)), including cerebral damage secondary to hypertension (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم), intracerebral hemorrhage (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد), acute cerebrovascular accidents (حادثة وعائية دماغية), cerebral arteriosclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية), dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture (Dissectie van cerebrale arteriën zonder ruptuur) and other ill‑defined cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten). Cerebral arteriosclerosis is further associated with multiple lacunar infarcts, illustrating the relationship between arterial stiffening and small‑vessel ischemic strokes. In the genitourinary

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2817
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System serves as the anatomical site associated with both other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease and اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder), indicating that abnormalities in the vascular network can underlie diverse cerebrovascular and renal vessel pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: (“تصلب الشرايين المخية”) and intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) contribute to chronic arterial stiffening, increase the risk of cerebrovascular accidents, and are associated with multiple lacunar infarcts (“خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد”). These sclerotic changes are mapped to occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, which in turn map to basilar‑artery stenosis (“تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي”) and to cerebral infarction. The broader category “Other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease” is the parent of “Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten,” which includes non‑infarct vascular brain injuries such as dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture, brain pathology caused by excess blood pressure, and arterial occlusion/stenosis that does not produce infarction. This category is linked to the Vascular System anatomic site, as well as to renal vessel disorder (“اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية”) and arterial ulceration (“تسلخ الشرايين”).
